course_info.title: Foreign Language I
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday - 1 hour per session

class_schedule.topic: Introduction: Language, Culture, and Global Communication
learning_objectives: Understand the role of language in international studies and diplomacy
Learn basic greetings, introductions, and classroom expressions
Begin developing phonetic awareness for the target language

lecture_notes.introduction: Language is the most direct bridge to understanding another culture. This course develops functional communicative competence alongside cultural awareness.
key_concepts: Language as a tool for international engagement
Phonetic system overview: sounds unique to the target language
Basic greetings and self-introduction
Numbers 1-20 and basic classroom vocabulary
Cultural context: politeness norms and register
lecture_notes.demonstration: Guided pronunciation practice with native speaker audio models
setup_requirements: Phonetic chart for the target language

review.value: The ability to hear, identify, and produce the distinct sounds of the target language, particularly those that do not exist in English
The level of formality in language use: knowing when to use formal vs. informal address is critical for cross-cultural communication in professional settings

class_schedule.topic: Everyday Conversations: Greetings, Introductions, and Courtesies
learning_objectives: Conduct basic introductions and exchanges of personal information
Use appropriate formal and informal address
Practice basic question formation and responses

lecture_notes.introduction: Mastering basic conversational exchanges is the foundation for all further language learning. Accuracy and cultural appropriateness are both important from the start.
key_concepts: Formal and informal greetings in the target culture
Asking and answering questions: name, origin, profession
Basic sentence structure: subject-verb-object or variations
Pronouns and verb conjugation: present tense basics
Cultural note: personal space, eye contact, and greeting customs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Paired practice: role-play introductions in formal and informal settings

review.value: Many languages distinguish between formal and informal 'you' (tu/vous, du/Sie, usted/tu) with important social implications; using the wrong form can be offensive or overly familiar
The target language's default word order and how it differs from English, forming the foundation for constructing all further utterances

class_schedule.topic: Daily Life: Describing Routines and Activities
learning_objectives: Describe daily routines using present tense verbs
Expand vocabulary for daily activities, food, and transportation

lecture_notes.introduction: Describing daily life requires expanding vocabulary and verb conjugation skills. This unit builds the language needed to talk about routines, preferences, and activities.
key_concepts: Regular verb conjugation: present tense patterns
Time expressions: clock time, days of the week, parts of the day
Daily routine vocabulary: eating, working, traveling, studying
Expressing likes, dislikes, and preferences
Cultural note: daily rhythms and meal customs in the target culture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Pair work: describing and comparing daily routines

review.value: The systematic modification of verb forms to indicate person and number (I go, you go, she goes), with patterns that differ significantly from English in most languages
Understanding that daily schedules, meal times, business hours, and social rhythms vary across cultures is essential for effective cross-cultural communication

class_schedule.topic: Navigating Spaces: Directions, Places, and Travel
learning_objectives: Give and follow directions in the target language
Use prepositions of place and location vocabulary
Practice survival language for travel situations

lecture_notes.introduction: Navigating physical spaces in another language is a practical survival skill. This unit develops the vocabulary and structures needed for travel and orientation.
key_concepts: Location vocabulary: buildings, streets, neighborhoods
Prepositions of place: in, on, near, between, behind
Giving and asking for directions
Transportation vocabulary and ticket purchasing
Cultural note: navigating cities in the target culture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map exercise: giving directions to a partner using a city map in the target language
setup_requirements: City maps in the target language

review.value: Words indicating spatial relationships (in, on, near, between) that often work differently from English and are essential for giving and understanding directions
The minimal language needed to navigate basic situations abroad: asking directions, buying tickets, ordering food, and requesting help

class_schedule.topic: Past Tense: Narrating Experiences and Events
learning_objectives: Form and use the past tense to describe completed actions
Narrate a sequence of events in chronological order
Expand vocabulary for travel, experiences, and storytelling

lecture_notes.introduction: Moving from the present tense to the past opens up an enormous range of communication: you can now tell stories, describe experiences, and discuss events.
key_concepts: Past tense formation: regular and irregular verb patterns
Time expressions for the past: yesterday, last week, ago
Narrative structure: sequencing events chronologically
Common irregular verbs in the past tense
Cultural note: storytelling traditions in the target culture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Storytelling exercise: narrating a recent trip or experience using past tense verbs

review.value: The rules for expressing past actions, which vary significantly across languages: some use auxiliary verbs, others modify endings, and many have multiple past tenses for different aspects
Using temporal connectors (first, then, after that, finally) to organize a story chronologically -- a fundamental skill for describing events in any language

class_schedule.topic: Cultural Competence: Expressing Opinions and Navigating Disagreement
learning_objectives: Express opinions, agreement, and disagreement politely
Use basic conditional and subjunctive forms
Navigate culturally appropriate discussion and debate

lecture_notes.introduction: Expressing opinions and navigating disagreement requires both linguistic skill and cultural sensitivity. This unit develops the language needed for respectful cross-cultural discussion.
key_concepts: Opinion expressions: I think, I believe, in my opinion
Agreement and disagreement phrases: graded from strong to tentative
Basic conditional: if X, then Y
Polite disagreement strategies in the target culture
Cultural note: directness vs. indirectness in the target culture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured debate: discussing a simple current event topic in the target language

review.value: Different cultures have different norms for expressing disagreement: some value directness, others prefer indirect communication, hedging, or silence -- knowing which is expected prevents offense
Structures expressing hypothetical situations (if I had time, I would study more) that enable more nuanced and polite communication

quizzes.topic: Basic Communication: Greetings, Introductions, and Daily Life

questions.question: Write a short dialogue (6-8 lines) in the target language in which two people meet for the first time at a professional conference. Include greetings, introductions, and questions about each other's work.
questions.answer: The dialogue should include formal greetings appropriate to the target culture, exchange of names and professional affiliations, at least two questions about each other's work, and proper closing courtesies. Correct use of formal register (e.g., vous/Sie/usted) is required.

questions.question: Describe your daily routine in 5-6 sentences in the target language, using at least 5 different verbs in the present tense.
questions.answer: Response should include 5-6 grammatically correct sentences describing daily activities, using at least 5 different correctly conjugated present-tense verbs, appropriate time expressions, and logical sequencing.

questions.question: Using a provided map, write directions in the target language from the train station to the university library (4-5 sentences). Use at least 3 different prepositions of place.
questions.answer: Response should provide clear, followable directions using correct prepositions of place (at least 3), imperative or polite command forms, and location vocabulary. Directions should be geographically logical.

rubric.key: Correct use of prepositions of place (3+)

questions.question: Write a paragraph (5-6 sentences) in the target language describing something you did last weekend, using the past tense. Include at least 4 different past-tense verbs.
questions.answer: Response should include 5-6 sentences narrating a past experience with at least 4 different correctly conjugated past-tense verbs, appropriate past-time expressions, and logical chronological sequencing.

major_assignments.title: Cultural Exploration and Oral Presentation
major_assignments.description: Research a cultural topic related to the target language's region and deliver a 5-minute oral presentation in the target language, accompanied by a written summary.
objectives: Research a cultural topic relevant to the target language region
Develop oral presentation skills in the target language
Demonstrate cultural knowledge and communicative competence
deliverables: 5-minute oral presentation in the target language
500-word written summary in the target language

criteria: Demonstrates genuine engagement with the culture
Responds to questions in target language
Competent oral presentation in the target language

weekly_quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on vocabulary, grammar, and communicative tasks

daily_participation.description: Active use of the target language in every class session

major_assignments.description: Cultural exploration oral presentation and written summary

midterm_examination.description: Written and oral exam covering Units 1-3

final_examination.description: Comprehensive written and oral exam covering all units

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is critical for language acquisition. More than 3 unexcused absences reduce the final grade by one letter step. More than 6 result in automatic failure.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late written work penalized 5% per day. Oral presentations cannot be rescheduled without medical documentation.
course_policies.collaboration: Pair and group work in class is collaborative and expected. All individual assessments must reflect your own language ability.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Translation software and AI language generators may not be used for graded work. Vocabulary tools and dictionaries are permitted.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to language learning platform, audio/video resources, and online dictionary for the target language.
